segment-text: Producers: Steven Soderbergh Paul Schiff
A NEW YORK APARTMENT
Spacious. Modernist furniture. Some black lacquer. A man's voice is heard. From his tone, we can tell he's used to people listening when he speaks.
The day I turned thirteen... TRACK into... THE BEDROOM...
BEN KALMAN sleeps.
...Actually, it was the night I turned thirteen, after the party and the envelopes and speeches and all that...

An alarm buzzes. Ben slowly sits up. It is a rare unguarded moment. He doesn't let anyone see him like this: untucked, groggy, wearing all of his 55-plus years on his face. It takes him a long moment to reach for his bathrobe and put it on.

...My father came into my bedroom, put a hand on my shoulder and said, "Ben, a lot of guys are gonna tell you a lot of shit. But what I'm gonna tell you applies across the board. Business, personal, whatever, what-haveyou...
He reaches into a bottle of children's aspirin, swallows one without water, and moves to the...
BATHROOM
Ben checks himself in the mirror. Slicks his hair with his hands. Pulls his robe tight.
"Son," he said, "fuck 'em where you find 'em, and leave 'em where you fuck 'em..."
Ben clears his eyes, splashes water on his face.
He was either a hundred per cent right or a hundred per cent wrong. I'm still not sure which.
Ben takes one more look at himself in the mirror and leaves the bathroom as Johnny Cash's version of Solitary Man begins to play.
CITY STREETS
CREDITS roll and the song continues as Ben moves through
the Upper East Side streets of Manhattan.

This is Ben as the world sees him: almost sixty but fighting it hard, he walks with the authoritative air of a man who usually gets his way. Note: Throughout, Ben always wears black Armani, black slacks, black jeans, black sweaters, black shoes. And if his hair isn't quite black, it's certainly not gray either. He and his Madison Ave. colorist makes sure of it once a month.
RIVERSIDE PARK...

Amidst the Bicyclists and joggers competing for space on the paths that run along the water, is a basketball court crowded with KIDS, PARENTS, BALLPLAYERS. SUSAN PORTER, twenty-seven, with big understanding eyes and a great laugh, stands with her eight-year-old son, SCOTTY. The two of them are looking up at... Which is caught between the rim and the backboard of a
A TEENAGER jumps up, knocks it through the hoop and hands it back to Scotty. Susan nudges her son.
Thanks.
He turns to Susan.
If Grandpa was here, he'd have gotten it for me, right?
He'd have tried.
Is he really coming this time?
She is about to answer when she sees Ben entering the park. There he is. Scotty sees him too.
Dad, Dad!
Fist pump.
Yes! Grandpa, over here! here, Grandpa!
But Ben doesn't seem to notice them. toward a bench on an opposite court. He moves past, As he walks by, he quietly speaks to Susan.
Don't call me that.
What?
He keeps walking. to keep up. Susan and Scotty move with him, trying
Don't call me Dad. And you, don't call me Grandpa. Not now.
What should I call you?
You can call me Dad.
No. That's what I call my dad.
Okay...Call me Captain Ben.
Susan turns her head.
Where's this one?
Ben gestures behind him.
Back there. You know what, don't look. You'll screw me up.
Now Susan stops and does an obvious scan of the park. SUSAN'S POV: A tawdry looking DYE JOB leans against the park's fence. She might be staring at Ben.
The Miami blonde?
I saw her checking me out as I walked through the fence. Here, take my arm. Maybe she'll think we're married.
Susan recoils.
Yuk.
I told you not to call me that.
How's it supposed to help you if she thinks you're my husband?
She's about 29, right?
Yeah.
At that age she's realistic, but still plenty vain.
Which means...?
She might have an affair with a married man, but there's no way she's gonna fuck a grandfather. Oh, excuse me Scotty. CONTINUED: (3)
He reaches down, picks up the little boy and gives him a big hug.
Can you put me on your shoulders Captain Ben?
Sure.
Ben tries to mask the effort it takes him to smoothly lift the boy onto his shoulders. When Scotty finally ends up safely perched, Ben whips around to show off for the fence leaning Girl.
Too late "Captain." three minutes ago.
Miami left Ben's face shows disappointment. And a hurt back. SCOTTY SWINGING... ...Between Ben and Susan as they walk along Broadway towards Artie's deli.
I have to go home,
A nap. They let go of Scotty who walks ahead of them, just out of earshot.
Jordan called me over at midnight. By the time we finished and I got back to my place-SUSAN You're pathetic.
What'd I do? I was sleeping. Phone rings, she's using her bedroom voice--
You're pathetic because you're dying to tell me about it. You're not going to take a nap. You don't take naps. You just want me to know you got laid.
It's not like I'm detailing the positions we did it in. A beat.
Susan waits for it.
She was on top. Then I was.
she turned over and-- Then
It's not normal for a man to speak to his daughter this way.
That's your husband talking. He's provincial. You're a grown women.
Gary didn't say anything.
I just--
You can handle it.
That's what you say about Jordan, right, to justify the fact that you're going to toss her in a dumpster when you're done.
She's got the power in this one. Not me.
No. She's got something you need. It's different.
And Ben smiles, real warmth behind it. Susan smiles too.
Why are you the one I can't con?
If you don't want me to reveal you to the world, you're coming inside, say hi to mom. No.
I can't stay.
So just say hi and go.
Your mother doesn't like it when I leave.
Never stopped you before.
Ben doesn't answer. He just puts his hand on Scotty's shoulder and, using the boy as a shield, enters...
P.J. BERNSTEIN'S DELI

Scotty breaks away from Ben and Susan and runs to the back of the restaurant into the arms of NANCY KALMAN, a mid-fifties woman who has kept it together without surgery, Botox or excess make-up. She notices Ben. Reacts. Her words may be sharp; her manner is not. She's actually glad to see him.
Ben. I'm surprised you have the time to drop in. What's the matter, no more divorcees left to bed on the east side?
That's not fair-You're right. widows.
I left out the
She turns to Scotty.
Sit down honey.
Scotty scoots in next to her.
Hi, Mom.
Susan bends over, kisses her mom and drops into a chair. Nancy gestures toward Ben.
Why is it still good to see him? (to Ben) Why is it still good to see you?
Cause you don't have to live with me anymore.
Well, join us.
Ben hesitates.
Just a quick hello, can't stay.
But as he says this, Ben's SPIES a TWENTY-YEAR OLD GIRL alone at an adjacent table. Furtively tries to make eye contact.
Well...I'll sit for a minute.
Ben takes a seat that gives him a clear view of the Twenty-Year Old Girl. Nancy laughs.
You're too old for that one Benny.
(covering) What are you talking about? Deny.
Always Deny.
They exchange a smile. they interact. There is an easiness to the way A waiter comes over to take Ben's order.
Can I get you anything?
I'll have a cheeseburger.
Have a turkey burger, Benny.
I'll have a cheeseburger.
It's only a matter of time until I get that phone call.
You've been expecting that call for thirty years...
Ben, catching himself, glances to make sure the Young Female Diner hasn't heard his age. She is reading a magazine and not paying any attention to them.
Dad, I'm taking Scotty to the Natural History this weekend. Gary has to work, so if you want to come along with us-BEN Can't.
Scotty looks up from his Gameboy.
Why not? A beat.
I'd like to, Scotty, but...
Ben hesitates.
Oh...You're gonna love this, Nance.
Out with it.
I'll be up at our alma mater this weekend.
Both women react.
No.
No way.
They finally convince you to come see the Kalman Pavilion?
Nope. Jordan and I are taking her daughter up for her college interview.
You're what?
I'm introducing her to the dean.
How'd you get roped into that?
Doesn't she have a father?
Apparently he has a life.
So why can't she take the girl herself?
Because of my 'relationship' with the dean.
Do you even like this Jordan? No answer.
The women work the tag team into high gear.
You're a sixty year old-BEN I am not-SUSAN --Close enough, dating a girl because of her father's connections.
A Waiter places food on table.
You needed your independence, right, that's why you left? Now you're a kept man.

Ben finally catches the eye of the Twenty-year-old Girl, smiles at her. BENS'S POV: The Twenty-year-old Girl smiles back at him, the way one smiles at a kindly older person on a park bench. No heat. Ben glances back at Nancy who seems to be looking right through him.
Ben?...
I've got to go.
Ben gives Scotty a kiss on top of his head.
I'll get you a check next week, Nance.
What about your burger?
Better for me that I don't eat it, right? Okay, Benny. there.
Okay.
Have fun up
Ben turns and walks out the door. Susan look to each other. Nancy, Scotty and
JORDAN'S BEDROOM...
...Which could be the centerpiece of an Architectural Digest spread.
I'm not going.

JORDAN LANGER, late 40s, beautiful, but in an uncompromising way, lies in bed. Ben stands over her. Jordan's daughter, ALLYSON LANGER, great looking, eighteen, with a cool Dalton detachment, leans against a wall. She is holding a box of tissues in her hand.
You promised.
I promised to go with you. But I'm sick.
I can't go.
So we'll reschedule-JORDAN Allyson can't reschedule her college interview just because I have the flu.
I tried to get you to take the
--I hate shots.
--And now you have the flu-Allyson cuts in.
--It's fine, Mom. I'll fly up there myself. In fact, I'd like that better. You see.
She doesn't even want my I'd cramp her style--
Allyson speaks under her breath.
Stomp on it you mean.
Jordan sits up with effort, turns to Ben.
That's exactly why I want an adult up there with her. Her style needs some cramping. Otherwise she might come home pickled.
Allyson snorts.
I stopped getting drunk in eighth grade, Mom.
Doesn't matter. You are not going up there alone. Give me a minute with Benjamin please.
Allyson hands her mother the tissues and leaves. tries not to let his eyes follow her out.
I don't think it's a good idea.
Didn't you retire from thinking?
And we're sure her father can't go with her?-JORDAN --He's in Macau, closing on some factory or something. Besides, he makes her too tense.
Jordan blows her nose. Coughs.
She's not going to be more relaxed with me. She doesn't even like me hanging around you.
What she likes doesn't matter. What's best for her does.
That's what my mother used to say as she tucked me into a giant purple snowsuit the first day of
I'd sweat through the thing on the bus to school, and everyone thought I wet my pants.
If Jordan even hears this intimate admission, she does not acknowledge it.
Ben, she puts on this big independent act because she thinks she's supposed to, but I know her. Please do this for me.
Jordan forces herself out of bed, crosses to her desk, takes a piece of paper out of the printer.
Before I forget, I just got the confirmation emailed to me. I printed it for you. They're sending a guy out tomorrow morning.
And it's all prepped?
It is. So think of it like a family weekend. First you'll go have the meeting my father arranged, and then you'll take my daughter up to her college interview. It's an order.
Jordan holds out the piece of paper.
Location isn't the only thing that matters, of course...
FOUR WAY INTERSECTION, SOMEWHERE IN NEW JERSEY - DAY
CLOSE ON: An artist's rendering of a state-of-the-art car dealership. A giant red sign in the foreground of the image says 'Kalman Motors.'
...But anyone can see, this is a hell of an open point.
PULL BACK from the mock-up to reveal... Ben striding with purpose across a large undeveloped lot. A step behind him is an automotive company exec, PETE
Nice traffic patterns.
Nice? Outstanding! With my private banking relationships, I'll be up and running in no time. And this zip code spends. Daughter graduates high-school, get her an SUV with six airbags.

Son moves into his own place, put him in a two-seater with some zip-PETE Your old flagship-BEN --Was north of here. Twenty-five miles. The name's still strong in this community-PETE --Our research certainly shows that the name is known in this community. But the negative numbers...
I'll sponsor the little league, the soccer, the school car washes. Negatives will disappear.

Mr. Kalman, I'm going to speak freely. Out of respect. I'm recommending that you get your dealership. Because the numbers make sense. Because my first job was in the finance department of one of your places. Because I grew up watching your commercials, 'I'm Ben Kalman, New York's honest car dealer.' And because you probably inspired me to go into this business in the first place. Well, Pete,
I'm honored to know--

--But I don't think my recommendation is going to do any good. The dealership development committee is not in the habit of granting open points to people who have brought the sort of public embarrassment to the industry that you did.
You get that recommendation sent up to the committee, I'll worry about getting them to sign off.
Fair enough.
Good. Let me show you where the service area will be.
They move off to another section of the lot.
FOUR WAY INTERSECTION, SOMEWHERE IN NEW JERSEY - LATER
Ben leans against his car, cell phone to his ear. mock-up loosely rolled beneath his armpit.
...It went as well as it could have, Jordan. Now, remind your father to speak to the three guys from Detroit, the younger ones who weren't there when I had my...
Should I lay his clothes out for him too? Or do you think he can handle it?
Okay, alright, Roger that,
Good. this.
Dismissed. Hey, I like
This puts Ben on comfortable terrain.
Well, we'll have to put you in a leather bomber and nothing else.
Now I can't wait for you to get back.
A beat.
Yeah. About this weekend...I'm sure Allyson doesn't really want me around, and-Total tone change from Jordan.

Of course she does. Just like my father wants to use fifty years of goodwill and connections, as well as his position on a board of directors, to get an admitted grifter a new dealership, after he almost tanked an entire company's reputation. A beat.
I'm on my way to the airport.
Believe me, she'll feel so much better with you there to guide her through the process.
JFK AIRPORT
Ben and Allyson wait in line to check in.
Just turn and leave. I can see how bad you want to.
He looks to her.
Go to Foxwoods for the weekend. Or Amagansett's great off season. Or wherever. Stay out of sight. C'mon, it's what you were hoping for the whole time.
A moment of relief plays on his face.
Just try not to catch anything you'll give my mother later. I don't want to have to take care of her.
Ben considers it. Shakes his head.
No. I don't lie to your mom. She almost lets him get away with it. Then can't.
I call bullshit, Ben.
What?
It means, ask me where I was the other night for drinks.
I thought you don't drink.
I said I don't get drunk.
Ask me.
Okay. Where were you drinking the other night?
The bar at the Warwick.
No reaction on Ben's face.
What night was that?
The night you hope it wasn't, when you were in the back booth with that Eastern European web special?
Oh. That was Eva. She's my niece. She was in New York on business. Why didn't you come over and say hi?
Allyson spools it out.
I was on my way to. But that's when I saw you slip your hand under her skirt. I thought maybe you'd dropped something, but then
she started squirming in a way
Allyson gets to the front of the line, hands her ID to the AGENT.
...So that's Gin.
Huh?
It means you win the hand.
I don't play cards. A beat.
Well, you didn't sell me out.
I'd cheat on her if she were my girlfriend. So have a good weekend, Ben.

Ben doesn't move. He just stands there and watches as Allyson bends down to hand her suitcase to the agent. ANGLE ON: A pair of BUSINESSMEN checking into first class appreciate her assets. ANGLE ON: Ben. He doesn't like it. Businessmen look away. Caught, the
Nope. Still coming with. I'm gonna do this for you. Not for your mother. For you. I know the school. I know the dean. I missed Susan's college tours, and I regret it. I was too busy back then...
The Agent hands Ben back his ticket. They start walking.
Yeah. Mom says you used to always be on MSNBC and CNN. That you had dealerships in every town in Jersey, Long Island and Connecticut.
Slight exaggeration.
How'd you fuck it up?
You never googled me?
You want me to believe what's online? Okay. I was just giving you a chance to-BEN I'm flying up there with you.
She doesn't answer.

I promise not to ruin your time. I'll show you around during the day, walk you to the interview so I can shake hands with the dean, smooth it all through for you. At night, I'll crash at the hotel and you can do whatever you want. And this time, I won't tell anyone.
Allyson stops, thinks about it for a second. eyes. Something changes between them. Meets his
And you won't treat me like a kid, right?
No snowsuit.
What? Forget it. kid.
Yeah, you're not a
A half-smiles comes to her face. to board the plane. And to his. They move
BEN'S ALMA MATER

The sun hits a rolling green campus in New England. FRISBEE GOLFERS, HACKY SACKERS, and DREADLOCKED STONERS share the quad with some actual SERIOUS-MINDED STUDENTS. Ben, black framed shades accenting his normal black on black, and Allyson, walk across the grass.
First time stepping back onto this quad since the day I finished.
You never even came up for homecoming? Not even the year after you graduated?

Year after I graduated I was moving six cars a month, on my way to my own shop. I wasn't going to miss a Saturday to spend it with a bunch of losers reliving glory they never had. When I was gone, I was gone.
Ben stops for a moment, looks around. His eyes land on a BENCH. He looks at it for a second longer then he should.
Ben?
That bench. It...Surprised it's still right here.
He shakes the memory off, and points into the distance.
Admission building is that way. Some kid was supposed to meet us, show us the way.
They head off in that direction.
I thought you were an involved alumni.
Alumnus. And that just means I've written some big checks.
Then how do you know the Dean and stuff?
They come to see me. Well, they used to when they knew they would walk out of my office with an endowed chair in the English department, or the ground breaking money for an arts center.
What about now?
They pay a courtesy call if they're in town to see someone else.
That's nice.
It's insurance. For when I can write the checks again.
A FRISBEE is about to hit Ben in the legs. out of the air. Looks to throw it back.
grabs it An IRATE STUDENT comes running towards him. Screaming.
HEY ASSHOLE. Let the big dog hunt, let the big dog hunt.
Ben doesn't get it.
Let the?-IRATE STUDENT Put the frisbee down. We're Frolfing. That was my drive. hole is over there.
He points to a maple tree 400 yards away.
You would've cost me a stroke if you threw it the other way. Already cost me a few yards by catching it.
It almost hit me. help. I was trying to
Yeah, well thanks but no, grandad.
The Irate Student sticks out his hand for the Frisbee. Ben almost gives it back. A slow smile comes over his face.
No. You called me an asshole. Now I'm gonna earn it.
Ben takes the Frisbee and whips it in the other direction. It sails high and gets stuck in a big oak tree. The Irate Student gets in Ben's face.
You motherfucker---'Ey.
There's a woman present. Short beat.
Dickwad.

Ben hardly moves. He turns his hips slightly, and lets his RIGHT FIST go. It lands in the Irate Student's stomach. The student doesn't go down though. Just stands there. Then he puts Ben in a ONE HANDED HEADLOCK. Ben's sunglasses fly off his face. Ben tries to free himself. Can't. The Irate Student grinds Ben's head under his arm a moment longer. The Student lets Ben go and pushes him away.
Like some arthritic punch was
Ben catches his breath, smooths his hair and turns to Allyson who has been watching with an admixture of horror and amusement.
I used to have a pretty good right.
He turns back to the Irate Student who is now standing inches from him.
What the fuck were you thinking, man?
Well, you know, standard boxing strategy: go to the body, weaken you, then go the head.
Ben throws a ROUNDHOUSE PUNCH that catches the Irate Student flush. This time he falls.
Seems like a sound strategy.

The Irate Student rears up and takes Ben down to the ground. They wrestle. Lots of grunting and groaning. Neither gains a clear edge. ANGLE ON: Ben is on the bottom. The Irate Student is about to punch him in the face when a big beefy hand comes down and lifts him off. The hand belongs to SGT. JOHN HAVERFORD, Campus Police. He is an enormous man, about Ben's age, who almost busts out of his uniform. He speaks with an accent that's Boston by way of Ireland.
Let's stop all this.
Ben scrambles to his feet. So is Sgt. John. The Irate Student is silent.
Officer. Ben Kalman. Dean Gitleson is expecting me-SGT. JOHN IDs.
Ben and the Irate Student hand ID to Sgt. John.
Someone want to tell me what happened here?
Misunderstanding. I'm willing to forget it if he is.
Sgt. John hands the student's ID back.
Get out of here.
Ben reaches out for his ID. The Sgt. pulls it back.
Not so fast. I want to know what you're doing on my campus.
Allyson answers.
Escorting me for my interview.
She your daughter?
She's not my daughter. old to be my daughter.
She's too This is not the right answer.
Too young to be anything else that won't get you arrested.
He goes out with my mother.
He tell you to say that?
No.

Let me see it.

Kalman. You're the donor. art center and all that.
I support the school every way I can.
So you're also the guy who sold my cousin in Connecticut on a leased Jeep that turned out to be financed. Cost him nine grand on the back end.
I had some unscrupulous managers. I take the blame for not watching them closer.
You do anything else untoward on my campus, you'll take more than the blame.
Sgt. John marches away. Allyson looks at Ben.
Wow. Smooth.
Ben leans down to pick up his sunglasses. As he comes up with them, an earnest looking student, DANIEL CHESTON, approaches.
Mr. Kalman. I'm Daniel Cheston. I am the Vice President of the student senate. Dean Gitleson sent me to find you. Are you okay, sir?
Ben takes a deep breath.
Better than that, Cheston. I'm great. I hadn't thrown a punch outside of a gym in years. Whoo!
The dean is ready for you. We should be getting over there.

EDWARD GITLESON, who is a career fund-raiser in an academic's clothes. He waits on the front steps of the
imposing admissions building, BRADLETON HALL..

This is Allyson Langer.

Hi.

Um...

Don't you want to see the pavilion?

Because my name's on the building?
Yeah. Another beat.
No.

You're walking a little funny. Should we get it checked out at the school clinic?
I don't do doctors. Not anymore.
Do you at least want to get yourself cleaned up? You can use my room.
Ben thinks about it for a moment.
Thanks.

They turn and walk off into the distance. Before they go out of frame, ANOTHER FRISBEE comes sailing very close to Ben. Ben sticks out a hand, looks like he might catch it, but then lets the Frisbee FLUTTER by.

Everything about you says it. Look at this room. If you have time to put hospital corners on your bed at college, you're spending your days the wrong way.
I am making the most of my-Ben cuts him off.

--They're always juniors. And you're still stuck trying to figure out how you lost...Laurie?

Diana.

is.
